By Rod James, Sr. Private Equity Editor

JP Morgan has started a new team to help private companies raise capital. Though a dominant force elsewhere, the bank's size is no guarantee of success in this rapidly growing, talent-constrained area of investment banking.

The team will advise owners of private businesses, mainly private equity sponsors, on raising capital for them without relinquishing control.

These options, such as moving companies into continuation funds or issuing preferred shares, have become increasingly popular in recent years as exits through traditional avenues have slowed, prompting banks to expand their offerings to clients.

"Historically, you only needed to think about an IPO or a sale as an exit. But there's much more you can do in the middle," Keith Canton told The Wall Street Journal, which first reported the news Jan. 16. Canton leads the private capital advisory team at JP Morgan and is co-head of equity capital markets in the Americas.

Continuation funds have become the preferred strategy.

Rivals Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs and Citi have had some form of in-house PCA business for several years.

According to conversations with PCA professionals, some of whom have been involved in the buildout of PCA teams at bulge-bracket banks, JP Morgan has a few things to think about.

The most active PCA groups are not big, full-service investment banks but boutique players, such as Evercore, Jefferies, Lazard and PJT Partners.

This dominance will make it hard for even JP Morgan to challenge on scale, said a New York-based managing director at a boutique bank who spoke under the condition of anonymity. They will need an angle to stop potential clients from taking their business to a seasoned player with a well-worn secondaries playbook.

Continuation fund deals combine elements of M&A and PE capital raising. While JP Morgan is extremely well placed for the former, it is less well equipped to do the latter.

With a small number of exceptions, the largest buyers in the secondary market have been the same for the past decade. Having strong relationships at these firms and understanding exactly what they are looking for in a continuation fund deal is vital.

Private Credit, Infrastructure, and Disciplined M&A Define December Activity 


Institutional investors closed out 2025 by deploying capital into private credit, infrastructure, and income-oriented real assets, while remaining selective on risk. Private credit fundraising was led by Oak Hill Advisors’ $17.7B senior lending raise and TPG’s $6.2B Credit Solutions Fund III, alongside continued momentum in infrastructure and digital assets. Notable allocator activity included Indiana Public Retirement System’s $1B allocation to Parametric Gold and New Hampshire Retirement System’s $100M commitment to KKR Global Infrastructure Investors V. Major transactions spanned technology, healthcare, and energy, including Armis ($7.8B), Merus ($8.0B), and the $6.5B Hornsea 3 Offshore Wind sale.

Download the full December 2025 Private Markets Review [cdp5.tracking.e360.salesforce.com] <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://cdp5.tracking.e360.salesforce.com/click?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..orlfZgDaEDBnLufYIrzYB01kyeyn7roS2P7FAlNihv4__;!!AHh8_Z5O2w!jNLVf1wlC8QRMy47WA6sjCRwa33caLJ0lhjyC_IB63bKSAO1xS4ClPSpfswjQfxuWaOwofGjLT7GoyVcIt0RVshIKi-a$>  to see how institutional capital is being deployed and how LPs are positioning for 2026. 	
________________________________



Transaction News Summary


Dealmaking opened the week slowly, even as capital formation heated up, with investors deploying capital into industrials, defense tech and AI ahead of the next wave of exits.

On the M&A front, Global Payments completed its long-awaited Worldpay acquisition while shedding its issuer solutions unit to FIS, sharpening its focus into a pure-play commerce platform processing nearly $4T in annual volume. Boyd Group followed with a $1.3B collision-repair consolidation play, snapping up Joe Hudson’s Collision Center to deepen its US footprint, while sponsor activity remained steady with Arlington Capital launching a new platform in defense and infrastructure engineering and B-FLEXION consolidating ownership of Radius Health.

Beneath the surface, the pipeline continues to build. Blackstone is preparing a potential £1B-plus exit of its UK single-family rental platform Leaf Living, Jacobs Capital is exploring a sale of outdoor gear brand Mammut and PGIM is weighing options for its Indian asset management business. Outside traditional buyouts, the NBA and FIBA are reportedly pitching a pan-European league that could value franchises at up to $1B apiece.

Funding markets stayed active. Apollo-backed QXO upsized its preferred equity raise to a hefty $3B to fuel an acquisition-led growth plan, while Atlantic Alumina secured $450M to expand US refining and gallium production. In venture, Dassault Aviation anchored a $200M Series B into defense AI specialist Harmattan, and security automation firm Torq joined the unicorn ranks with a $140M Series D.

Evergreen funds require a different kind of diligence 	
By Hilary Wiek, Principal Analyst, Fund Strategies

Fewer than 20 drawdown funds in history have reached $25 billion, yet a handful of evergreen funds have already grown far larger. Despite being marketed as familiar extensions of private-market strategies, their structures create fundamentally different incentives around fundraising, valuation, and liquidity—ones that can meaningfully alter both manager behavior and investor outcomes.

For those seeking access to private markets through drawdown funds, there are some questions and considerations that should be explored before diving in. Just because you can access a fund or a manager you believe to be a top performer, doesn't mean you should.

While some are excited to finally have access to private markets, they should go in with their eyes wide open. Large asset managers are even more excited, embracing the opportunity to get even larger.

Why Greenland's future is important to Silicon Valley

By Leah Hodgson and Andrew Woodman, PitchBook News

Greenland should be a geopolitical footnote, given its tiny population and distance from venture capital centers. Yet, renewed threats to the island’s sovereignty—and its resources—have highlighted Greenland’s importance to the US, including Silicon Valley.

A part of the Kingdom of Denmark, Greenland was once again drawn into a diplomatic row this month after President Donald Trump reiterated his desire for the US to acquire the Arctic territory. The fresh challenge, which followed the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro, carries both advantages and risks for America.

For VC investors and tech executives, Greenland’s importance doesn’t stem from any consumer demand or startup density, but from its inputs.

Central to Greenland’s appeal to Silicon Valley is its swathes of rare mineral resources that power everything from smartphones to satellites. A 2023 study by the Geological Survey of Denmark and Greenland found that 25 of the 34 minerals deemed critical were present in the country.

Greenland is among the top 10 nations globally in terms of reserves that have remained largely untapped, and the melting ice due to climate change is making access easier.

Currently, China dominates the rare minerals market. More than 60% of these elements globally are mined in the Asian nation. Beijing effectively controls the world’s supply and has the power to stymie US technological advancements if it imposes restrictions.

The US is already seeking to strengthen supply chains as it looks to cut its dependence on China. Reducing its reliance is becoming more critical in the age of AI.

The large-scale computing infrastructure that underpins AI relies on elements such as neodymium and dysprosium for cooling, insulation and durability. Reduced access would mean higher costs, slower deployment and less efficient infrastructure, weakening the competitiveness of US AI companies on which investors have bet hundreds of billions of dollars.

Beyond its rare mineral reserves, investors are eyeing up Greenland as a potential hub for the ever-growing need for data centers. The island, which is roughly the size of Western Europe and has a population of 57,000, would be a prime location for data centers. Furthermore, its cold weather conditions offer an advantage. Energy costs account for approximately 60% of a data center’s total lifetime operating expenses, primarily due to the need for cooling.

Evergreen funds require a different kind of diligence 	
By Hilary Wiek, Principal Analyst, Fund Strategies

Fewer than 20 drawdown funds in history have reached $25 billion, yet a handful of evergreen funds have already grown far larger. Despite being marketed as familiar extensions of private-market strategies, their structures create fundamentally different incentives around fundraising, valuation, and liquidity—ones that can meaningfully alter both manager behavior and investor outcomes.

For those seeking access to private markets through drawdown funds, there are some questions and considerations that should be explored before diving in. Just because you can access a fund or a manager you believe to be a top performer, doesn't mean you should.

Our latest analyst note [url6380.news.pitchbook.com] <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://url6380.news.pitchbook.com/uni/ls/click?upn=u001.d4n73BrX4Cuylw7SpSWIrf6GFa-2BaqM7ONnvsnswouzVrxOoIhsmCCo7-2FWeE7pgNWumJkZ2u1zO2TtosJkHa2-2Bw-3D-3D1cur_QXnTW6f9jV7ots26-2Fd0iCEYeiPzd5cXg8j8hhainmZFHt41V35rYgQg11oOqvj8g33gSCTSuCfqKjpEXvf-2Fp-2B-2Fqjz0hGIYULI3tuY4sui9Q6I8HLjWQSab8tQj4-2BkKkY5X9wzM-2FX4hMfDDu-2FUX6e3ooWAc1uvQxvg5C6IABekU1sv6EEwxcPWi9HVrGvKgFQEpR0xfE0IUBM3hOFBpK2HWMzvGY-2FSYN-2FesfFl1n1qsFdQE94qJP638a-2BCDqzcTfDetqCdTqGtH6GnvltmrN46FzxF3YwbH7WeZnpvgaXx6c-2BcPmyKEOzCboeLj-2Bzq2aOomdfVwr6vvswJP7RK0UgpLJjtaB51ZN1J7jaTE5Rulllw3V-2F4TjUWcWJpOeidQS-2BcOKI-2Bo6U8CzgiYmAyxjh4WYHa6wnDDSQgnX-2FPrsusm31InhQ0weyq3-2FsQP2cEnpqOZuJbP-2FW7DFouSMgAgFDVlMuOnG3d2oQkiD5p-2FE1kSVaBCb4-2FAPSotYTExUg0hJ35u8SWMAbMbt9xCSNto-2FxnqUNzA-2FfPE6yB6YHiVK3bQI-3D__;!!AHh8_Z5O2w!gc84k7iMg19uTVeVkZcYlg0sChVf7QGWH8GTGO3mprmS8jWEgSVS3XDzqAY7pSZfyIcDUf08Ax7OXUN0ozB9dXcsYoqvEYAP4mo$>  is meant to help investors in semi-liquid funds and other interested parties consider diligence questions unique to the space. It contains dozens of questions to ask evergreen fund managers, using the same "6 P" framework introduced in earlier research on fund due diligence—covering people, philosophy, process, portfolio construction, performance and pricing.

Why Greenland's future is important to Silicon Valley

By Leah Hodgson and Andrew Woodman, PitchBook News

Greenland should be a geopolitical footnote, given its tiny population and distance from venture capital centers. Yet, renewed threats to the island’s sovereignty—and its resources—have highlighted Greenland’s importance to the US, including Silicon Valley.

A part of the Kingdom of Denmark, Greenland was once again drawn into a diplomatic row this month after President Donald Trump reiterated his desire for the US to acquire the Arctic territory. The fresh challenge, which followed the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro, carries both advantages and risks for America.

For VC investors and tech executives, Greenland’s importance doesn’t stem from any consumer demand or startup density, but from its inputs.

Central to Greenland’s appeal to Silicon Valley is its swathes of rare mineral resources that power everything from smartphones to satellites. A 2023 study by the Geological Survey of Denmark and Greenland found that 25 of the 34 minerals deemed critical were present in the country.

Greenland is among the top 10 nations globally in terms of reserves that have remained largely untapped, and the melting ice due to climate change is making access easier.

Currently, China dominates the rare minerals market. More than 60% of these elements globally are mined in the Asian nation. Beijing effectively controls the world’s supply and has the power to stymie US technological advancements if it imposes restrictions.

The US is already seeking to strengthen supply chains as it looks to cut its dependence on China. Reducing its reliance is becoming more critical in the age of AI.

The large-scale computing infrastructure that underpins AI relies on elements such as neodymium and dysprosium for cooling, insulation and durability. Reduced access would mean higher costs, slower deployment and less efficient infrastructure, weakening the competitiveness of US AI companies on which investors have bet hundreds of billions of dollars.

Beyond its rare mineral reserves, investors are eyeing up Greenland as a potential hub for the ever-growing need for data centers. The island, which is roughly the size of Western Europe and has a population of 57,000, would be a prime location for data centers. Furthermore, its cold weather conditions offer an advantage. Energy costs account for approximately 60% of a data center’s total lifetime operating expenses, primarily due to the need for cooling.

From the heart of India’s startup ecosystem, Brian Mac Mahon shares an update from Expert Dojo’s ongoing India Tour.

Expert Dojo is three cities into a six—ultimately seven—city journey across India, completed over fourteen high-intensity days. From investor dinners to one-on-one founder meetings, the tour has brought together some of the country’s most driven entrepreneurs and investors. 

With Delhi and Jaipur completed and Mumbai, Bangalore, and Chennai ahead, the momentum continues. 

The goal is clear: to invest in 500+ Indian founders over next 5-7 years. 

And this is just the beginning. 

  	

India Tour Highlights


Jaipur | Where the Journey Took Flight

 [link.mail.beehiiv.com] <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.Cqk7eAGHmn3go5ELIfN-52U1qAyWZiMdQsOwFBkEMMiib-PB9PcKF8cCAhMsRWqR29KwrSOIC_wZ_ZKZjDlUPe4QDB1kkxCGY5oijZsZDOvcY0sfjbdLJlc-haC7hd9o9rDldnEcGBv3Up9BRJ14mFwpkr6wf3R2HTlnDYgG_4ZziRhCGXIPU6egtJXkSzS6sEFEYulZqVX9ZEqGHfTxyCZYARbsr4onjsXjR7ie7C8XjvrzsosCB7X_LC6Yr6o-pZcH3KavDZbNhM7jGA0Ax469ykmCpNj_2tdDHrxzz5IMVWO-iYF65HMMi2YFLQKzIT-dzkmtYU9PQ_aR9qjypw/4n6/I-Dn-5V5RN25Ld0eDZX3Lw/h2/h001.bj-PwydRN0LxX2G92CxmlQN_tT3xxCKxaKrDLYjG580__;!!AHh8_Z5O2w!nmxnEA1KkyhW7hA1A1OfndKfxxqVorJ08e5b6p57zDxo2Lx1jF1aQctPmWjyfEzrfeO9SlqEOah82_FiVyLZfc9HWtNr$> 	 
We kicked off the Expert Dojo India Tour in Jaipur, and it couldn’t have been a stronger start. 

High energy. Sharp conversations. Long-term thinkers in the room. 

This wasn’t about noise — it was about real capital, real intent, and real alignment. 

Starting our India journey at the top wasn’t luck — it was clarity. 

The conversations we had reaffirmed one thing: founders here are thinking bigger, earlier, and more globally than ever before. 

A big thank you to everyone we met at TiE Global Summit (TGS) [link.mail.beehiiv.com] <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.Cqk7eAGHmn3go5ELIfN-52U1qAyWZiMdQsOwFBkEMMgQS02spPKuaVK4dMP8s46AaaNcK6MWIakJu9mcR8j2A5iwEATr-u8OxeaoP37XYamv-mfRYEGU7Pf3Rx2m-rrzME82Uw8y7yo7gt6hIVFWOJInrekEh5BNcM9W3a6YVEJ6Jh1r8ycR8wr-zZIw9742Vf1lsJOvZj3nGyWVSSJgwwi_z2Xrej-Q-wRvFTQlD827pEcULFLx_PUGoAML6Dv_rgYJVS2r-McmjNK2qQbIS9WzN0nSQEQV-sfb7eiDub1bMc_hl7oFp4UBqjfmYzFX1zhkWbtdLuR6tgkdBZ2A8Q/4n6/I-Dn-5V5RN25Ld0eDZX3Lw/h3/h001.Mip2uhWMKJcKT4FewCGcRC6lN1Wc2IFtESs73lsroZ8__;!!AHh8_Z5O2w!nmxnEA1KkyhW7hA1A1OfndKfxxqVorJ08e5b6p57zDxo2Lx1jF1aQctPmWjyfEzrfeO9SlqEOah82_FiVyLZfWYzPbv_$>  — founders, investors, family offices, operators, and ecosystem builders who showed up with conviction and purpose. 

Jaipur didn’t just start the tour. 

It set the tone. 

And from here on — we’re only building momentum. 🚀 

  	

Ahmedabad | The Making of What Comes Next

 [link.mail.beehiiv.com] <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.Cqk7eAGHmn3go5ELIfN-52U1qAyWZiMdQsOwFBkEMMiib-PB9PcKF8cCAhMsRWqRxSNAl9WPqREyS9k-0pn8ut9KVm1u88Jf2LOyQV2cuL_G83IdA8e6KzPDnBne2WRNjlSjnFqOxKwepY9WoNv7MicgIl3sRHyNYAAuzhMY5j509c15mFRzHIr3zHxS-rMUcOZjr8BCBynqY21DR09hc7GX6Y2oX4FG46KDp3nRFHBLsWRgn_WZ8crKdoTkt2fstZAz0FrTSmcqOsUXqL9dZ4yfMJ1iD2kSQ5m4f6Dxv5eFhY-NXapIFM5OdDTyORn9pyz8Uh53KO3PzxQicOEdVA/4n6/I-Dn-5V5RN25Ld0eDZX3Lw/h4/h001.voPmCXHf2CDFkpMahiEAk20kNR0nwgSWyPB8RJWDAug__;!!AHh8_Z5O2w!nmxnEA1KkyhW7hA1A1OfndKfxxqVorJ08e5b6p57zDxo2Lx1jF1aQctPmWjyfEzrfeO9SlqEOah82_FiVyLZfQ1tKrvp$> 	 
Stop #2: Ahmedabad felt less like a checkpoint and more like a story coming together. 

We began the day at IIT Gandhinagar, engaging in honest, grounded conversations with founders and students about what it actually takes to build a unicorn.
No hype. No shortcuts. Just lessons from the trenches, clarity on mistakes, and the importance of thinking global from day zero. 

Next, we headed to GIFT City — a powerful reminder of where India’s financial ambition is headed.
Capital, policy, structure, and scale — all converging to support the next generation of world-class companies. 

We closed the day with an intimate Family Office gathering & dinner.
Fewer slides. More substance. Long-term capital in the room, thoughtful questions on the table, and conversations rooted in patience, trust, and building companies meant to last decades. 

Ahmedabad wasn’t an ending.
It was a signal of what’s coming next. 

  	
We’re here to build.
We’re here to connect.
We’re here to create momentum — city by city, table by table. 

Next stops loading… ✈️

Across India, Family Offices, HNIs, and UHNIs are asking the same question:
How can we participate in venture capital without turning it into speculation?

Early-stage investing looks very different when capital is permanent, relationships matter, and investment horizons span decades—not quarters. 


What These Closed-Door Gatherings Focus On


*	Structuring venture exposure responsibly for Family Offices 

*	Direct investments, co-investments, and FOF-style participation 

*	Risk management, governance, and downside protection 

*	Accessing global deal flow without chasing hype 

*	Building India–US capital corridors rooted in trust, not transactions 

There are no pitches, no panels, and no selling—only honest, off-the-record conversations among capital partners who think in decades.

Transaction News Summary


Dealmaking landed squarely in multibillion-dollar territory as infrastructure assets, sports investing and AI-driven strategies garnered headlines on a busy day.

The headline transaction came from Europe, where Triton agreed to sell Swedish traffic management platform Ramudden Global to I Squared Capital in a deal reportedly valuing the business near €2.5B. The sale highlights continued demand for essential infrastructure assets with predictable cash flows, even as sponsors look to monetize scaled platforms.

KKR stayed busy on multiple fronts. The firm struck a roughly $1B deal for Arctos Partners, securing exposure to professional sports ownership and secondaries, while also committing an additional $1.5B of equity to European data center developer Global Technical Realty alongside new partner Oak Hill Capital, doubling down on AI-driven digital infrastructure.

Tech-led consolidation continued unabated as Mobileye agreed to acquire humanoid robotics startup Mentee Robotics for about $900M, extending its reach into embodied AI.

In the pipeline, Eli Lilly is circling a potential $1B-plus deal for Ventyx Biosciences, while TPG is in advanced talks to acquire Optum UK, underscoring ongoing consolidation across healthcare technology.

Funding rounds remained sizable despite the lighter calendar. Elon Musk’s xAI closed a $20B Series E to scale next-generation AI infrastructure, Rakuten Medical raised $100M to advance late-stage trials and European fintech Payhawk is exploring a $100M-plus round that could double its valuation to approximately $2B.

Read on for a rundown of all the latest transaction news.

Subject: PrimeBeds: Rapid Expansion - Next Round Opening
Body:
Hi Corner Ventures,
I'm Mukesh, founder of PrimeBeds. We've closed our pre-seed and are
building India's first standardized short-stay PG/homestay chain — a
segment with clear demand but completely unbranded, inconsistent
supply.
We're now opening a small allocation in the next round before we move
into rapid expansion. If this is something you'd like to explore, I'm
happy to share the deck if you want an early look.

Regards,
Mukesh
Founder, PrimeBeds
